video_title: Nike Earnings Surpass Expectations as Q2 Comes to a Close | The Close 6/30/2026
gist: Bloomberg's The Close caps the strongest quarter for the S&P 500 since 2020 (chips posting their best quarter ever), then dissects Nike's fiscal Q4 beat (EPS $0.72 vs $0.14 y/y, revenue $10.97B, China down 12%) amid Elliott Hill's turnaround and a new Pfizer-sourced CFO. Guests span BlackRock fixed-income flows, Treasury central-clearing structure, an RBC bull case on beaten-down Microsoft, and Jack Lew on sanctions and the Iran nuclear MOU.

themes.theme: Fixed-income flows and the front-end tilt

insights.claim: Despite yields on the long end being attractive (30-year Treasury ~4.9%), the vast majority of new fixed-income money is going to the short end, with ~$64B into ultra-short-duration funds in 2026 while long-dated funds saw ~$500M of outflows.
insights.speaker: Steve (Global Head of iShares, BlackRock) / Katie Greifeld
insights.so_what: Investors are allocating for income and avoiding duration despite a functional long-end return, signaling persistent distaste for term risk.
insights.quote: THE BIGGEST DEMAND IS FOR ULTRA SHORT DURATION FIXED INCOME FUNDS. ABOUT $64 BILLION HAS BEEN POURED INTO THAT MATURITY BUCKET.

insights.claim: The 20-plus-year part of the Treasury curve (e.g. TLT) is actually outperforming the 1-to-3-year part, a counterintuitive fact given money is still flowing to the short end.
insights.speaker: Steve (BlackRock)
insights.so_what: Flows are chasing yield/income rather than total return, so long-end price performance is being ignored by allocators.
insights.quote: THE 20-PLUS-YEAR PART OF THE CURVE, WE LOOK AT SOMETHING LIKE T.L.T. IS OUT-PERFORMING THE ONE TO THREE-YEAR PART OF THE CURVE THAT MIGHT SURPRISE PEOPLE.

insights.claim: Market-implied inflation at the short end has plummeted, with the two-year breakeven falling from a high around 3.4% to roughly 2%, so short-dated TIPS look attractive for investors still worried about inflation.
insights.speaker: Steve (BlackRock)
insights.so_what: Markets are looking past energy/conflict-driven price pressure, creating a potential entry point in short-dated inflation protection.
insights.quote: LOOK AT TWO-YEAR BREAK EVEN THEY WENT FROM A HIGH AROUND 340 ALL THE WAY DOWN TO ABOUT 2%.

insights.claim: June was a record month for investment-grade issuance driven partly by Magnificent Seven tapping primary markets for AI buildout, yet spreads stayed tight (IG in the mid-70s bps, high-yield a couple hundred above), showing the market absorbed the supply.
insights.speaker: Steve (BlackRock)
insights.so_what: AI-driven IG supply is being digested without spread widening, so credit remains a functioning funding channel for hyperscaler capex.
insights.quote: JUNE WAS A RECORD MONTH FOR I.G. ISSUANCE. THERE'S TALK THAT THAT'S CAUSING SOME CROWDING OUT... BUT REALLY, THE ISSUANCE HAS BEEN FAIRLY WELL-RECEIVED.

insights.claim: European investment-grade and high-yield credit is an overlooked opportunity that is performing well even relative to US investment grade, a story BlackRock focuses on in active strategies.
insights.speaker: Steve (BlackRock)
insights.so_what: A non-consensus allocation idea for credit investors seeking relative value outside the crowded US market.
insights.quote: IF YOU LOOK AT EUROPEAN INVESTMENT GRADE AND EUROPEAN HIGH-YIELD, IT'S DOING PRETTY WELL EVEN COMPARED TO U.S. INVESTMENT GRADE... A STORY THAT I THINK HASN'T BEEN LOOKED AT THAT CLOSELY.

themes.theme: Treasury market structure and central clearing

insights.claim: Central clearing interposes a CCP between the two counterparties on a Treasury trade, so participants no longer face each other, which reduces counterparty credit risk and can bring more participation into the market during times of stress.
insights.speaker: Nate (Head of Market Structure, BNY; former head of domestic markets, NY Fed)
insights.so_what: The clearing mandate structurally re-plumbs the Treasury market and could improve liquidity precisely when it matters most.
insights.quote: IT INTERPOSES A C.C.P. IN BETWEEN THE TWO COUNTERPARTIES OF THE TRADE. SO YOU DON'T FACE YOUR COUNTERPARTY ANYMORE YOU NOW FACE THE C.C.P.

insights.claim: The end-of-June repo central-clearing deadline is a bigger deal than the December cash-Treasury deadline because it involves more complex infrastructure change, and the SEC has signaled it granted an extra year and now expects industry to show progress.
insights.speaker: Nate (BNY)
insights.so_what: Repo clearing is the harder operational lift and the more likely candidate for future friction or extension debate.
insights.quote: AN EVEN BIGGER... I WOULD ARGUE IS AT THE END OF JUNE FOR THE REPO MARKET AND THAT IS MORE COMPLEX BECAUSE IT INVOLVES MORE INFRASTRUCTURE CHANGE.

insights.claim: The Treasury market is ~$30 trillion today and CBO forecasts have it growing to over $50 trillion in the next 10 years, making absorption capacity and intermediary resilience the central structural questions.
insights.speaker: Nate (BNY)
insights.so_what: Supply growth of this magnitude puts dealer intermediation capacity at the center of future market functioning and stress dynamics.
insights.quote: IT'S A $30 TRILLION MARKET TODAY... YOU HAVE... THE MARKET GROWING TO BE OVER $50 TRILLION IN THE NEXT 10 YEARS.

insights.claim: Loosening of the leverage ratio and liquidity ratio restrictions could over time help intermediaries facilitate more transactions in the Treasury market amid growing supply.
insights.speaker: Nate (BNY)
insights.so_what: Regulatory relief is a lever policymakers are pulling to expand dealer balance-sheet capacity ahead of the supply wave.
insights.quote: YOU HAVE SEEN SOME CHANGES IN REGULATIONS, LOOSENING OF RESTRICTIONS AROUND THE LEVERAGE RATIO... THAT COULD OVER TIME HELP INTERMEDIARIES.

themes.theme: Microsoft: AI-vs-software fear and the buy case

insights.claim: Microsoft's worst month in 26 years and four straight quarters of S&P underperformance stem from being hit on both sides: fears AI will replace application software, and impatience over high capex with no sign of slowing.
insights.speaker: Rishi (Managing Director, Software Equity Research, RBC Capital)
insights.so_what: Frames the exact narrative overhang a contrarian buyer must believe is overdone.
insights.quote: THEY ARE GETTING HIT ON THAT NARRATIVE THAT AI IS GOING TO REPLACE APPLICATION SOFTWARE... AND ON THE OTHER SIDE, THERE IS GROWING IMPATIENCE OVER HIGH CAPEX.

insights.claim: The cost of replacing Microsoft's Office suite (Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, Teams, OneDrive, SharePoint) yourself would be at least 10x more expensive even for the most capable organization, making obsolescence-by-AI fears overblown.
insights.speaker: Rishi (RBC Capital)
insights.so_what: The replacement-cost moat argument underpins the case that AI won't cannibalize Microsoft's core franchise.
insights.quote: THE ACTUAL COST OF THAT WOULD BE A MINIMUM 10 TIMES MORE EXPENSIVE IF YOU DID IT YOURSELF EVEN IF YOU ARE THE MOST CAPABLE ORGANIZATION IN THE WORLD.

insights.claim: Office Copilot is finally improving after Cynthia stepped back into product/engineering in November, adding 5 million paid subscribers last quarter with engagement now on par with Outlook and more expected this quarter.
insights.speaker: Rishi (RBC Capital)
insights.so_what: Copilot traction is the leading indicator RBC watches to validate the turnaround, and it is now inflecting positively.
insights.quote: THEY ADDED 5 MILLION PAID SUBSCRIBERS LAST QUARTER... THEY TALKED ABOUT OFFICE COPILOT ENGAGEMENT BEING ON PAR WITH OUTLOOK.

insights.claim: The market is unhappy that Microsoft is allocating first-party Azure capacity to its own apps like Office Copilot and GitHub Copilot (no revenue association) rather than to third-party customers including OpenAI and Anthropic, though RBC argues this is the right long-term call.
insights.speaker: Rishi (RBC Capital)
insights.so_what: Capacity-allocation choices are pressuring near-term Azure optics and explain part of the underperformance, but may build durable owned-app value.
insights.quote: ALLOCATING FIRST PARTY CAPACITY TO APPLICATIONS LIKE OFFICE COPILOT, GITHUB COPILOT WHERE THERE IS NO REVENUE ASSOCIATION. VERSUS TWO THIRD-PARTY CUSTOMERS INCLUDING OPENAI AND ANTHROPIC.

themes.theme: Sanctions, the dollar and the Iran nuclear MOU

insights.claim: The Iran agreement reached weeks ago is only a memorandum of understanding that does not detail how the toughest issues (removing highly enriched material, an inspection regime, timing of sanctions relief) would be resolved.
insights.speaker: Jack Lew (former US Treasury Secretary, former Ambassador to Israel)
insights.so_what: Investors treating the MOU as a resolved deal are overreading it; substantial and market-relevant negotiation risk remains.
insights.quote: THE AGREEMENT REACHED... IS A MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING. IT DOESN'T LAY OUT IN ANY DETAIL WHAT A NUCLEAR AGREEMENT WOULD LOOK LIKE.

insights.claim: At moments of maximum stress during the Iran conflict there was a rise in non-dollar transactions and use of dollar-avoidance plumbing, but flows returned to the dollar within days once tension eased because nothing else offers comparable depth and liquidity.
insights.speaker: Jack Lew
insights.so_what: De-dollarization is real at the margin under stress but not yet structurally displacing the dollar's reserve role.
insights.quote: AS SOON AS THE TENSION WENT DOWN, EVEN FOR A DAY OR A WEEK, IT CAME BACK TO THE DOLLAR. THERE'S SIMPLY NOTHING OTHER THAN THE DOLLAR THAT'S AVAILABLE IN THE DEPTH AND LIQUIDITY.

insights.claim: Imposing pain on Iran in some ways increased their endurance for pain, and the US reaction to higher gasoline prices weakened America's ability to withstand a Strait of Hormuz closure, showing sanctions must be used with care around counterparty leverage.
insights.speaker: Jack Lew
insights.so_what: Energy-price sensitivity is a binding constraint on US coercive leverage, relevant to oil and geopolitical risk pricing.
insights.quote: THEY ALSO SAW THE U.S. RESPONSE TO HIGHER GASOLINE PRICES, WEAKENED OUR ABILITY TO WITHSTAND A CLOSURE OF THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ.

insights.claim: Iran now appears to have gained increased control over the Strait of Hormuz coming out of the war and MOU, with ambiguous language each side reads differently on whether Iran can charge tolls to reopen it.
insights.speaker: Jack Lew
insights.so_what: A structurally more Iran-controlled chokepoint is a lingering risk premium for global energy shipping.
insights.quote: WHETHER THEY CHARGE A FEE OR NOT, THEY CERTAINLY HAVE INCREASED THEIR KIND OF CONTROL OVER THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ.

themes.theme: Quarter-end market recap: rotation and concentration

insights.claim: The S&P 500 rose ~15% (18.5% total return) for the quarter, its best since 2020, the Nasdaq gained over 21%, and semiconductors posted their best quarter ever and are on track for their best year ever if gains hold.
insights.speaker: Romaine Bostick / Carol Massar / Tim Stenovec
insights.so_what: Sets the risk-on backdrop: chip-led melt-up with the Dow up 13% and Russell 2000 up ~21% for the quarter.
insights.quote: BEST QUARTER FOR THE S&P 500 SINCE 2020 AND MAYBE EVEN ELEVATED FURTHER BACK. BEST QUARTER EVER FOR CHIP STOCKS.

insights.claim: Market leadership is broadening in all directions, not just away from Mag 7: emerging markets up almost 23% YTD, small caps up 22%, and value up over 16% while growth is not even up 1% YTD.
insights.speaker: Tiffany McKee (CFO/CIO, Pivotal Advisors)
insights.so_what: The AI narrative masks a pronounced value-over-growth and EM outperformance that argues for genuine diversification.
insights.quote: VALUE IS UP OVER 16% YEAR TO DATE VERSUS GROWTH WHICH THROUGH LAST WEEK IS NOT EVEN DOING 1% YEAR TO DATE.

insights.claim: US equities are over 60% of global market cap, creating dangerous concentration in portfolios, so investors should be deliberate about regional diversification within the 60% equity sleeve of a 60/40.
insights.speaker: Tiffany McKee (Pivotal Advisors)
insights.so_what: A concrete risk-management flag: passive S&P indexing embeds extreme US concentration that rebalancing should address by region.
insights.quote: U.S. GLOBAL MARKET CAP IS OVER 60% SO THERE IS A LOT OF CONCENTRATION IN PORTFOLIOS RIGHT NOW.

insights.claim: In the March 2026 market turmoil, opportunistic/private credit, real assets, and defensive (hedge-like) equities were what kept endowment client portfolios afloat, and manager selection favored smaller (not the biggest) private-credit firms.
insights.speaker: Tiffany McKee (Pivotal Advisors)
insights.so_what: Non-core satellite allocations provided downside protection during the March shock, informing where to seek resilience.
insights.quote: WHEN I LOOK AT THE AREAS THAT KEPT OUR CLIENTS AFLOAT IN THAT CRAZINESS WAS PRIVATE CREDIT, OPPORTUNISTIC CREDIT, REAL ASSETS, AND SOMETHING WE LIKE TO CALL DEFENSIVE EQUITIES.

insights.claim: To play AI in a diversified way, own the creators (foundation companies), the builders (infrastructure), and the adopters, with copper as a key metal to power AI rather than concentrating in Nvidia and Microsoft.
insights.speaker: Tiffany McKee (Pivotal Advisors)
insights.so_what: A three-layer AI framework points investors toward second-order beneficiaries like industrial metals.
insights.quote: WE WANT TO OWN THE CREATORS... THE BUILDERS... AND... THE ADOPTERS AND SO THAT IS WHERE COPPER FITS IN.

insights.claim: S&P earnings could exceed 20% EPS growth in 2026, a number rarely achieved outside recession-recovery periods, and if cyclical and non-AI sectors contribute more it could replay the 2021 post-pandemic profit boom.
insights.speaker: Romaine Bostick (citing Citadel data)
insights.so_what: Frames the bull case that the rally is earnings-driven, not purely speculative, with room for cyclical breadth.
insights.quote: THE S&P OVERALL COULD EXCEED 20% E.P.S. GROWTH IN 2026. THAT'S A NUMBER RARELY ACHIEVED OUTSIDE OF RECESSION RECOVERY PERIODS.

themes.theme: Sector rotation, telecom disruption and single-name moves

insights.claim: Telecom stocks fell for a second straight day (AT&T down over 5% after falling 6.3% the prior day) on news that SpaceX and Charter Communications are in executive-level talks on a consumer phone offering that could challenge the big three wireless providers.
insights.speaker: Tim Stenovec
insights.so_what: A satellite/cable entrant threatens the core mobile business of incumbent carriers, a fresh disruption vector for telecom valuations.
insights.quote: SPACEX AND CHARTER COMMUNICATIONS ARE HAVING EXECUTIVE LEVEL TALKS ON A CONSUMER PHONE OFFERING THAT COULD CHALLENGE WIRELESS PROVIDERS.

insights.claim: Call-center outsourcers Concentrix (down 11.2%) and Teleperformance fell as Concentrix cut its FY2026 revenue outlook and warned customers are cutting back, amid fears human-handled requests will be replaced by AI.
insights.speaker: Tim Stenovec
insights.so_what: AI substitution risk is now showing up in guidance cuts for labor-arbitrage BPO firms, a concrete AI-displacement casualty.
insights.quote: CONCENTRIX CUT ITS REVENUE OUTLOOK, WARNED ITS CUSTOMERS ARE CUTTING BACK ON SPENDING.

insights.claim: Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix announced plans to build two chip-making plants apiece in South Korea totaling about $500 billion, boosting chip-tool names like KLA and ASML (up ~5.6%).
insights.speaker: Carol Massar
insights.so_what: A massive Korean fab investment cycle underpins the semiconductor capital-equipment rally.
insights.quote: SAMSUNG ELECTRONICS AND S.K. HYNIX ANNOUNCED PLANS TO BUILD TWO CHIP MAKING PLANTS APIECE IN SOUTH KOREA FOR A TOTAL OF ABOUT $500 BILLION U.S.

insights.claim: Analyst top calls flagged a downgrade of Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley to underperform on heavy leverage risk (advising 'boring banks'), a Rocket Companies initiation at $21 as a future dominant real-estate portal, and a SanDisk target raise to $3,000 from $1,700 on new customer agreements.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg anchor (Top Calls segment)
insights.so_what: Captures the sell-side positioning skew: cautious on levered banks, constructive on mortgage fintech and memory chips.
insights.quote: STICK TO BORING BANKS.

insights.claim: Blackstone is selling ~$3.5B of data centers to Digital Realty not to exit the sector but to recycle capital into other hyperscale projects, reinforcing rather than reducing its data-center position.
insights.speaker: Norah Mulinda (Bloomberg TV markets correspondent)
insights.so_what: A capital-recycling read on the deal signals continued conviction in AI infrastructure, not a top in data-center demand.
insights.quote: BLACKSTONE IS NOT DITCHING DATA CENTERS. IF ANYTHING, THEY ARE REINFORCING THE POSITION... ESSENTIALLY RECYCLING THEIR CASH FLOW.

insights.claim: Nike's fiscal Q4 beat with EPS of $0.72 (vs $0.14 a year earlier) and revenue of $10.97B above the ~$10.8B estimate, with gross margin at 39.2% and inventory up only ~0.2%.
insights.speaker: Romaine Bostick / Katie Greifeld
insights.so_what: A clean beat against low expectations, but the market reaction was muted as guidance and China remained the swing factors.
insights.quote: EPS, $.72 A SHARE VERSUS 14 CENTS YEAR-OVER-YEAR... THAT REVENUE NUMBER DOES APPEAR TO BE RELATIVELY ABOVE ESTIMATES.

insights.claim: Greater China revenue was $1.3B, down 12% year-over-year, but that was much better than the high-teens decline that had been expected, while North America grew ~2.7%.
insights.speaker: Katie Greifeld / Poonam Goyal (Bloomberg Intelligence)
insights.so_what: China is still the key overhang, but a less-bad print eases the worst fears while North America carries the recovery.
insights.quote: GREATER CHINA REVENUE, $1.3 BILLION. THAT IS DOWN 12% YEAR-OVER-YEAR... MUCH BETTER THAN THE HIGH TEENS DECLINE THAT WAS EXPECTED.

insights.claim: Nike's gross-margin increase this quarter is not indicative of the go-forward trajectory because the company still has a lot of discounting to do to clear inventory, and the benefit is largely one-time and tied to tariff reform.
insights.speaker: Poonam Goyal (Bloomberg Intelligence)
insights.so_what: Warns investors not to extrapolate the margin beat; underlying markdown pressure remains.
insights.quote: THE INCREASE WE SEE HERE, IS NOT INDICATIVE OF A GROSS MARGIN TRAJECTORY. THEY DO HAVE A LOT OF DISCOUNTING TO DO. THAT IS ALL ONE TIME.

insights.claim: CFRA argues analysts are underpredicting Nike's earnings power, seeing over $2 EPS achievable fairly easily in a decent year and $3 within reach over the next two to three years, with running up over 20% last quarter.
insights.speaker: Zachary Warren (equity analyst, CFRA)
insights.so_what: The bull case rests on an earnings-power rerating that consensus is missing, driven by product momentum.
insights.quote: WE THINK NIKE CAN DO OVER $2 IN EARNINGS PER SHARE. PRETTY EASILY IN JUST A DECENT YEAR WE THINK 3 IS IN REACH IN THE NEXT TWO TO THREE YEARS.

insights.claim: Nike historically sees revenue up ~8% in FIFA World Cup years versus 5-6% in non-World-Cup years, giving CFRA higher expectations for the coming fiscal year.
insights.speaker: Zachary Warren (CFRA)
insights.so_what: A quantified seasonal tailwind supports the near-term revenue setup for the turnaround narrative.
insights.quote: TYPICALLY THEY SEE REVENUES UP 8% IN WORLD CUP YEARS COMPARED TO 5% TO 6% IN NON-WORLD CUP YEARS.

insights.claim: In M Science data, Nike is winning in World Cup apparel but Adidas is well outpacing Nike in soccer jerseys, and footwear remains the segment where Nike is relatively challenged.
insights.speaker: Drake McFarlane (research analyst, M Science)
insights.so_what: Alternative data shows the World Cup lift is uneven and Adidas is the sponsor beneficiary, tempering the Nike demand read.
insights.quote: EVEN THOUGH NIKE HAS DONE WELL, WE HAVE SEEN ADIDAS WELL OUTPACE THEM IN SOCCER JERSEYS.

insights.claim: Salomon has grown substantially in US footwear, showing a shoe's popularity is not tied to its intended use case (its hit was originally a 1970s indoor soccer shoe), and low-profile styles are re-emerging over chunky designs across Salomon, Adidas Samba and Nike's retro Moon Shoe.
insights.speaker: Drake McFarlane (M Science)
insights.so_what: A design-cycle turn toward low-profile shoes plus new upstart competitors reshapes where footwear share will accrue.
insights.quote: YOU CAN SEE EARLY HINTS THAT THE CHUNKY WAVE MIGHT NOT BE HERE FOREVER. FOR LIFESTYLE, I THINK LOW-PROFILE IS THE WAY.

insights.claim: Nike now competes not just with other shoe brands but for discretionary dollars against everyday essentials as cost-of-living rises, meaning even a strong brand must repeatedly prove its worth to a stretched shopper.
insights.speaker: Wendy Liebman (CEO/Chief Shopper, WSL Strategic Retail)
insights.so_what: Reframes Nike's addressable competition as the whole household budget, a macro-consumer headwind to premium footwear demand.
insights.quote: NOW YOU'RE COMPETING WITH EVERY DAY ESSENTIALS AND NOT JUST ANOTHER PAIR OF SNEAKERS.

themes.theme: Nike's new CFO and the AI-driven leadership playbook

insights.claim: Nike named David Denton (from Pfizer) as CFO to replace Matthew Friend, starting in August; industry experience is seen as less important than knowing how to run a business of scale with heavy capex and a global supply chain.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg senior editor (CFO Briefing)
insights.so_what: The unconventional pharma-to-apparel CFO hire signals Nike prioritizes scale/supply-chain management over sector pedigree.
insights.quote: WHAT MATTERS IS IF YOU KNOW THE BUSINESS OF SCALE THAT HAS A CERTAIN... CAPEX AND A WIDENING SUPPLY CHAIN AROUND THE GLOBE, WHICH DAVE HAS.

insights.claim: An executive-search CEO argues the most interesting thing about Denton is Pfizer's AI-native transformation, which supposedly drove close to $5 billion in efficiency improvement, and expects similar finance-function AI automation at Nike.
insights.speaker: Jeff Christian (founder/CEO, Christian & Timbers)
insights.so_what: Positions the CFO hire as an AI-driven cost-and-efficiency lever, with FP&A/finance headcount reduction as the near-term result.
insights.quote: SUPPOSEDLY THEY'VE DONE CLOSE TO 5 BILLION IN EFFICIENCY IMPROVEMENT AS A RESULT OF AI TRANSFORMATION.

insights.claim: CEO runways have collapsed from three-to-five years to about three, and given AI's pace, Elliott Hill likely has only about another year to turn Nike around before succession questions arise; Nike has already cut ~1,400 tech jobs.
insights.speaker: Jeff Christian (Christian & Timbers)
insights.so_what: The turnaround clock is short, raising the odds of further executive change and AI-driven restructuring if performance lags.
insights.quote: I WOULD SAY ELLIOTT PROBABLY ONLY HAS ANOTHER YEAR, TO TURN THE COMPANY AROUND BEFORE I WOULD START THINKING ABOUT A NEW CEO.
